title: Chapter 16: Elif's Dilemma
content: Three weeks. That's how long it takes to build something you can't put down.

Elif stared at the spreadsheet she'd made at 2 AM because she couldn't sleep and because spreadsheets were how she processed the world. The numbers were almost comical. In twenty-one days of running @capybaradaily, she'd earned more than two months of twelve-hour shifts at Mount Sinai. More. Not kind of more. Not a little more. *Significantly* more.

She'd paid off the credit card. The one that had been hovering at $3,400 like a vulture over her credit score. Gone. Zero balance. She'd ordered new scrubs — the fancy kind, the figs with the stretchy waistband that didn't pinch after six hours on her feet. They arrived yesterday. She'd held the packaging like it was a gift from a relative who actually knew her.

The student loan payment was current for the first time since she'd graduated. Not the loan itself — the loan was a phone number with a dollar sign in front, a number so large it had long ago stopped feeling real and started feeling like weather, something that just existed and couldn't be changed. But the payment. The monthly minimum. Current.
And there was money left over.

Elif had never had money left over. Leftover was a concept that belonged to other people — people with stocks, people with planners, people who said things like "I'm going to max out my Roth IRA this year" like that was a sentence that could come out of a human mouth without laughing. Leftover money was a fiction. Except now it was sitting in her checking account like it had always been there, like this was normal, like she'd always been the kind of person who could order takeout without doing mental arithmetic about whether it would mean skipping lunch for the rest of the week.
She should have been happy. She *was* happy. The spreadsheet made her happy. The credit card balance made her happy. The scrubs made her stupidly, disproportionately happy.
But the spreadsheet was only one document on her phone, and the other document was her DMs, and her DMs were a problem.

The message came from a verified account. Green checkmark and everything. Elif had learned that green checkmarks were like sirens — beautiful, compelling, and absolutely going to crash you into rocks if you followed them.

*Hey! We're with Meridian Podcast Network. We're developing a limited series about viral animal accounts and the humans behind them. We'd love to feature @capybaradaily — specifically the household dynamic. Your roommate situation is SO compelling. Would love to chat about interviewing the people who live with the capybara. Let's talk numbers?*
Elif's stomach dropped. Not fluttered. Not dipped. Dropped, like someone had cut the cables on an elevator.

The people who live with the capybara. That was her. That was Maya. That was Dave. That was their apartment, their bathroom, their sacred tub, their actual real lives that they had not volunteered to share with the entire internet.
She read the message again. Then again. Then she closed the app, opened it, and read it a third time, because apparently she was a masochist.

She found Dave in his room, headphones on, hunched over his audio interface like a doctor over a patient. He was eq-ing something, surgical and precise, his face in that particular mode where the rest of the world had been surgically removed from his awareness.
"Dave."
He held up one finger. Adjusted a dial. Listened. Adjusted again. Satisfied, he pulled the headphones around his neck. "What's up?"
"Would you do a podcast about Chloe?"
Dave didn't even blink. "Absolutely not."
"They're offering fifteen thousand dollars."
Dave blinked exactly once. "Absolutely."
Elif laughed, but it came out wrong — too sharp, too short, like a cough. "That's the problem."
"What is?"
"Everyone has a price."

Dave leaned back in his chair. The headphones cable stretched. He looked at her with an expression she'd never seen on him before — not quite pity, not quite concern, but something adjacent to both. "Elif. That's not a problem. That's just being a person."

"Is it?" She sat on the edge of his bed. His room was the tidiest in the apartment, everything in its place, cables coiled with an almost pathological neatness. Even his mess was organized. "Because I didn't ask. Before I posted that first clip, I didn't ask anyone. I just — I saw Chloe in the tub, and she looked so *content*, and I thought people would like it. And they did. And now there's fifteen thousand dollars on the table and I'm sitting here doing the math about whether it's worth it to — to what? Sell Chloe's bathroom? Sell our apartment? Sell the fact that Dave would do a podcast about his roommate for fifteen grand?"
"I would," Dave said mildly. "Student loans."

"I *know*. That's what I'm saying. I have them too. Mine are — " She stopped. The number was too embarrassing. It was too large and too real and saying it out loud would make it a fact she couldn't take back. "They're a lot. They're like a phone number with a dollar sign. And this — " She gestured vaguely at her phone, at the invisible DM, at the entire absurd economy that had materialized around a capybara in a bathtub. "This is the first time they've felt manageable. Even a little. Even temporarily."
Dave was quiet for a long time. Then he said, "You should talk to Chloe."
"She's a capybara, Dave."
"She's Chloe. She's always Chloe."

The bathroom door was open. It usually was — Chloe had an open-door policy about the tub, which was ironic given that the tub was the most private space in the apartment. Elif knocked on the doorframe anyway, because you knocked before entering someone's space even if they were a capybara and couldn't respond.

Chloe was in the tub. Of course she was. She was submerged up to her nostrils, eyes half-closed, the water barely rippling. The bathroom smelled like eucalyptus because Maya had bought eucalyptus salts two days ago and Chloe had seemed — well, not *grateful*, because capybaras didn't do gratitude in any recognizable way, but she had stayed in the tub for six hours straight, which was either a review or a dissertation.
Elif sat on the bath mat. It was damp. She didn't care.

"Hey," she said. "So. There's a podcast network that wants to do a show about you. About us. The apartment. The whole thing. They want to interview us. All of us. They want to come here, into the apartment, and film, and ask questions about what it's like to live with — "
She stopped. What it's like to live with you. Like Chloe was a curiosity. A specimen. A thing that happened to other people.

"Fifteen thousand dollars," she said quietly. "Which is — that's a lot. That's two months of rent. That's a student loan payment with money left over. That's the difference between surviving and actually being okay."
Chloe's eyes opened a little more. One ear turned toward Elif. Listening.

"I didn't ask you," Elif said. "Before the first post. I just — I saw you, and you were so peaceful, and I thought people should see that. And now there are four hundred thousand people who've seen that, and brands want to sponsor it, and podcasts want to talk about it, and I never once asked if that was okay. Because you couldn't answer. Because you're a capybara. And it was easier to not ask than to figure out what asking would even look like."
The water was still. Chloe's breathing was invisible, submerged.

"And now I'm sitting here kind of asking, but also kind of not, because you still can't answer the way I need you to, and I don't know if that's an excuse or a reason or just — just me making myself feel better about something that was never mine to take."

She ran out of words. Not metaphorically — literally. The sentence ended and there wasn't another one behind it. She'd said the thing and now there was nothing but the sound of water and the distant hum of Dave's monitors through the wall.

Chloe opened one eye. Fully. Looked at Elif with a steadiness that felt human, which was impossible, which was projection, which was exactly the kind of thing Elif would do — project humanity onto an animal because it made her feel less alone.
Then Chloe hummed.

It was different from her usual hum. Slower. Lower. It started in her chest — Elif could see the vibration, the way the water rippled outward in concentric circles — and it dropped, settled, found a register that wasn't the morning hum or the contentment hum or the there-you-are hum. This was something else. This was the hum of someone who had been listening and was now responding in the only language they had.
Elif's chest loosened. The tightness she hadn't realized was there — behind her sternum, wrapped around her ribs — released. Not all at once. Slowly. Like a knot being picked apart rather than cut.
Chloe shifted in the tub. Lifted her head, just slightly. Turned toward the bathroom door, which was open, which was always open, which let the warmth out and the sound of the apartment in.
Then, very deliberately, Chloe nudged the door with her nose.
It swung shut. Not hard. Not dramatic. Just — closed. Gently. The click of the latch was almost nothing.
Closed door. Private space.
The message was unmistakable.
*Sometimes the answer is just no.*
Elif sat on the bath mat for a long time after that. She could hear Chloe on the other side of the door — the low hum continuing, muffled now, held close. Not for Elif. Not for anyone. For herself.
She got up. Her legs were stiff. The bath mat had left a pattern on her thigh.
She walked to her room. Opened the app. Typed a reply to Meridian Podcast Network: *Thank you for the offer. We're not interested at this time.*
Sent.

Then she did something she hadn't done since she'd started the account. She logged off. Not just closed the app — logged off. Switched off notifications for 24 hours. Put the phone face-down on her nightstand.

Tomorrow she would go to the hospital. She would wear her new scrubs and stand for twelve hours and do the work she'd trained for. She would be present. Fully present. Not half-in the app, half-in the shift, refreshing between patients.

It was strange. The numbers were still there — the loan, the rent, the credit card that was finally, mercifully empty. The podcast money was gone, which meant the loan was still a phone number and the rent was still due and the math was still math.

But as Elif lay in bed, staring at the ceiling, listening to the muffled hum through two walls and a closed door, the numbers felt lighter. Not smaller. Lighter. Like they were still the same size but she was carrying them differently.
Chloe had shut the door.
Elif had listened.
And the right choice had been made without a single word.
